title: Security clearance tracking

compliance.assurance_rationale: NO Sikkerhetsloven §5-3 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO Sikkerhetsloven §5-3 (Risk assessment and documentation of security level) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.2: Security clearance tracking.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.26.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=hr_security OR index=identity sourcetype IN ("clearance:hr","okta:user_lifecycle") earliest=-7d
| eval upn=lower(coalesce(user, profile_login))
| stats latest(clearance_level) as clr, latest(_time) as hr_ts by upn
| join type=left upn [| search index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624,4634) earliest=-7d | eval upn=lower(user) | stats latest(_time) as last_auth by upn]
| where clr IN ("None","Revoked") AND last_auth>hr_ts
| table upn, clr, hr_ts, last_auth
description: Joins authentication success events to HR clearance attributes to highlight accounts active after clearance expiry or role change.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO Sikkerhetsloven compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes hr_security, identity, wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security, clearance:hr, okta:user_lifecycle. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=hr_security`, `index=identity`, `index=wineventlog`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`, `sourcetype=clearance:hr`, `sourcetype=okta:user_lifecycle`.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations). Key fields: `clr`, `hr_ts`, `last_auth`, `upn`, `upn]`.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=hr_security OR index=identity sourcetype IN ("clearance:hr","okta:user_lifecycle") earliest=-7d
| eval upn=lower(coalesce(user, profile_login))
| stats latest(clearance_level) as clr, latest(_time) as hr_ts by upn
| join type=left upn [| search index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624,4634) earliest=-7d | eval upn=lower(user) | stats latest(_time) as last_auth by upn]
| where clr IN ("None","Revoked") AND last_auth>hr_ts
| table upn, clr, hr_ts, last_auth
```

Understanding this SPL

**Security clearance tracking (Sikkerhetsloven)** — Joins authentication success events to HR clearance attributes to highlight accounts active after clearance expiry or role change.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: hr_security, identity.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=hr_security, index=identity, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **upn** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `stats` aggregates events by **upn**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where clr IN ("None","Revoked") AND last_auth>hr_ts` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(All_Changes.status) as agg_value from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - agg_value
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Security clearance tracking (Sikkerhetsloven)** — Joins authentication success events to HR clearance attributes to highlight accounts active after clearance expiry or role change.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: clr, hr_ts, last_auth, upn, upn].

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for joins authentication success events to hr clearance attributes to highlight accounts active after clearance expiry or role change. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
